title: What to know before using Angelica dahurica (Bai Zhi)?
intro: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese Angelica or Dong Quai, is a herbaceous plant native to Northeast Asia. It has a long history of use in traditional Chinese medicine for treating various ailments such as headaches, inflammation, and respiratory infections. This plant thrives in cooler climates and well-drained soil, often found in mountainous regions. A. dahurica is characterized by its tall, sturdy stems and large, compound leaves, as well as its small, yellowish-white flowers that bloom in umbels. Its medicinal properties are attributed to its roots, which contain various bioactive compounds including essential oils, coumarins, and furanocoumarins, known for their anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antibacterial effects.
medicine_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese angelica or dong quai, has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries due to its numerous medicinal benefits. It is known to alleviate various ailments, including headaches, inflammation, and respiratory infections. The plant's roots and rhizomes are used in herbal remedies, and its essential oil is used in aromatherapy. Angelica dahurica has been shown to have antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties, making it a valuable addition to any natural medicine cabinet.

Angelica dahurica contains various constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant's essential oil is rich in compounds such as β-phellandrene, α-pinene, and ligustilide, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects. The plant also contains coumarins, ferulic acid, and polysaccharides, which have been shown to have antioxidant, antiviral, and immunomodulatory effects.

Angelica dahurica can be prepared in various ways, depending on the desired medicinal effect. The plant's roots and rhizomes can be dried and ground into a powder, which can be added to hot water to make a tea. The essential oil can be extracted from the plant and used topically or inhaled. Angelica dahurica can also be used in tinctures, capsules, and extracts. It is often combined with other herbs, such as licorice root and cinnamon bark, to enhance its medicinal effects.

While Angelica dahurica has many medicinal benefits, it can also have side effects, especially if taken in large doses or for extended periods. Some common side effects include digestive issues, such as nausea, bloating, and diarrhea. The plant can also cause skin irritation and phototoxicity, so it is important to avoid prolonged exposure to sunlight after using it topically. In rare cases, Angelica dahurica can cause allergic reactions, such as hives and difficulty breathing. It is important to speak with a healthcare provider before using Angelica dahurica, especially if taking other medications or have a history of allergic reactions.

There are several precautions to consider when using Angelica dahurica. It should not be used during pregnancy or breastfeeding, as it can stimulate menstruation and lead to miscarriage. The plant can also interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and sedatives, so it is important to speak with a healthcare provider before using it if taking other medications. Additionally, Angelica dahurica should be stored in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight, as exposure to light and heat can degrade its medicinal properties. It is important to follow dosage instructions carefully and speak with a healthcare provider if experiencing any adverse effects.
horticulture_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Dahurian Angelica, is a robust and hardy plant that thrives in cooler climates. It prefers partial shade to full sun and well-drained soil. This plant can grow up to 6 feet tall and 3 feet wide, so it requires ample space to spread its roots and branches. It is native to Northeastern Asia, particularly in China, Korea, and Russia, and can tolerate temperatures as low as -20°F. However, it is crucial to protect the plant from extreme heat and dryness during the summer months.

When planting Angelica dahurica, it is recommended to start the seeds indoors about 8-10 weeks before the last expected frost. The seeds require a period of cold stratification to break their dormancy, so it is advisable to refrigerate them for 2-3 weeks before sowing. Once the seedlings have grown to about 2-3 inches tall, they can be transplanted outdoors in a location with the appropriate sunlight and soil conditions. It is essential to space the plants at least 2 feet apart to allow for proper growth and air circulation.

Angelica dahurica requires regular watering and fertilization to maintain its health and vigor. It is recommended to water the plant deeply once a week, providing enough moisture to penetrate the root zone. However, it is crucial to avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot and other fungal diseases. A balanced fertilizer can be applied in the spring, following the package instructions, to provide the necessary nutrients for growth. It is also beneficial to mulch around the base of the plant to retain moisture and suppress weeds.

Harvesting Angelica dahurica can be done in the second year of growth, once the plant has established a strong root system. The roots are typically harvested in the fall, after the first frost, when the plant's medicinal properties are at their peak. To harvest, carefully dig around the base of the plant, taking care not to damage the roots. After harvesting, the roots should be washed thoroughly and cut into small pieces before drying. The leaves and stems can also be harvested in the summer, when they are at their most aromatic.

Angelica dahurica is relatively resistant to pests and diseases, but it can be susceptible to aphids, spider mites, and powdery mildew. To prevent infestations, it is recommended to keep the plant healthy and well-maintained, providing adequate water and nutrients. If pests are present, a natural insecticidal soap can be used to control the population. To prevent powdery mildew, it is essential to avoid overcrowding the plants and providing good air circulation. Regularly inspecting the plant and removing any affected leaves or stems can also help prevent the spread of the disease.
botany_desc: Angelica dahurica, also known as Chinese Angelica or Dong Quai, is a plant species belonging to the family Apiaceae, which includes carrots, parsley, and celery. It is a biennial or perennial herb that can grow up to 2 meters tall. Its taxonomy is well-established, with its scientific name, Angelica dahurica, given by the German botanist, Friedrich Ernst Ludwig Fischer in 1806. It is classified under the genus Angelica, which contains around 60 species, mostly from temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.

Angelica dahurica has a distinctive morphology that makes it easily recognizable. It has a thick, stout stem that is hollow and grooved. The leaves are large, compound, and bi- or tripinnate, with a sheathing base. The flowers are small, white or greenish-white, and are arranged in large, compound umbels. The fruit is a dry, two-parted schizocarp, which splits into two mericarps at maturity. The roots are thick, fleshy, and aromatic, with a strong, characteristic odor.

Angelica dahurica has several variant names, including A. dahurica var. formosana, A. dahurica var. urticifolia, and A. dahurica var. sinensis. These variants differ in their morphological and anatomical characteristics, such as leaf shape, flower size, and fruit structure. For example, A. dahurica var. formosana has larger leaves and flowers than A. dahurica var. dahurica, while A. dahurica var. urticifolia has a more hairy stem and leaves. However, these variants are not always clearly distinguished, and some botanists consider them as part of a single, variable species.

Angelica dahurica is native to Northeast Asia, including China, Korea, Japan, and Russia. It prefers moist, shady habitats, such as forests, meadows, and riverbanks. It is commonly found in the mountainous regions of China, such as the provinces of Heilongjiang, Jilin, Liaoning, and Inner Mongolia. It is also cultivated in other parts of China, such as the Yangtze River Delta and the Pearl River Delta, for its medicinal and culinary uses. In Korea, it is found in the northern provinces of Hamgyeongnam-do and Kangwon-do, while in Japan, it is distributed in the northern island of Hokkaido and the northern part of Honshu.

Angelica dahurica has a biennial or perennial life-cycle, depending on the environmental conditions. In its first year, it produces a rosette of leaves and stores energy in its thick roots. In its second year, it bolts and produces a tall stem, flowers, and fruits. The seeds are dispersed by wind and water, and can remain viable in the soil for several years. The plant also reproduces vegetatively, through its rhizomes, which can produce new shoots and form colonies. The roots and rhizomes are harvested in the autumn of the second year, or in the spring of the third year, when the active constituents, such as essential oils, coumarins, and polysaccharides, are at their highest levels.
